blocks.text: The Book of Commandments, 1833
By December 1832, the new volume of revelations—entitled A Book of Commandments—was being typeset on the Church’s press in Independence, Missouri.

Before Oliver Cowdery and John Whitmer carried the revelations from Ohio to Missouri for printing, Joseph Smith received a revelation intended as a preface to the Book of Commandments. It began: “Hearken, O ye people of my church, saith the voice of him who dwells on high, and whose eyes are upon all men; yea, verily I say: Hearken ye people from afar; and ye that are upon the islands of the sea, listen together.” This preface appeared in the March 1833 issue of The Evening and the Morning Star and by that time had already been typeset as chapter 1 of the Book of Commandments.

In May 1833, the Star ran the revealed appendix to the new volume of scripture, the paper’s editors predicting, “The book from which this important revelation is taken, will be published in the course of the present year.” Work on the book was progressing more slowly than originally expected, and the editors apologized to their fellow Church members: “We beg their forbearance, and solicit an interest in their prayers, promising to use our exertions with all our means to accomplish this work.”

The project had proven daunting, and the members of the Church’s Literary Firm had to modify their expectations. The Hiram, Ohio, conference that proposed the publication in 1831 voted to produce ten thousand copies. When Joseph Smith was in Missouri in 1832, the firm reduced the total copies in the first print run to a more reasonable three thousand copies. It also authorized William W. Phelps, Oliver Cowdery, and John Whitmer to review the revelations and “select for printing such as shall be deemed by them proper, as dictated by the Spirit.”

But Joseph Smith retained the right to review and approve what they had done. By July of 1833, William Phelps and his colleagues had most of the new volume printed: five large sheets, each of which would fold into thirty-two pages for the Book of Commandments. They had sent copies of some of the sheets to Joseph for review, and he had sent back a letter with his proofreading corrections. The letter told the printers not to worry about binding the book. Demand for it was running high, and he didn’t want the book kept “too long from circulation.” Success seemed imminent.

On July 20, 1833, however, Missouri vigilantes—upset about an article in the Star—attacked the two-story building that doubled as the print shop and the home of the Phelps family. The attackers forced open a door on the first story and found William’s wife, Sally, who was home at the time with their children, including a sick baby. The mob drove them out and proceeded upstairs to the print shop. They threw the printing press out the second-story window and tore the building completely to the ground.

As they were tearing down the building, one vigilante walked out carrying some of the large printed sheets of revelations. “Here are the Mormon commandments,” he announced triumphantly, dumping them. Fourteen-year-old Church member Mary Elizabeth Rollins and her twelve-year-old sister, Caroline, peered from a fence corner during the attack.

“When they spoke about them being the commandments,” Mary later wrote, “I was determined to have some of them. So while their backs were turned, prying out the gable end of the house, we ran and gathered up all we could carry in our arms.”
As Mary and Caroline turned to flee, two of the mob jumped down from the house and yelled at them to halt. With the prized pages in their arms, the young women were not about to stop.

“We ran as fast as we could,” Mary wrote, “through a gap in the fence into a large corn field, and the two men after us.” She remembered, “We ran a long way in the field, laid the papers on the ground, then laid down on top of them.”
Tense moments followed. “The corn was very high and thick. They hunted all around us, but did not see us.”
When the men finally gave up, Mary and Caroline found Sally Phelps, wife of William, and gave her the pages. “Subsequently Oliver Cowdery bound them in small books and gave me one,” Mary recalled.
Mary’s copy survived and is now a prized volume in the collections of the DeGolyer Library of Southern Methodist University.

Besides the Rollins sisters, others also rescued pages, and some two and a half dozen copies of A Book of Commandments are known to have survived into the twenty-first century. Because of the book’s rarity and desirability to collectors, its price has escalated over time. Originally, the printers planned to offer the volume at “from 25, to 50 cents a copy.” One and three-quarters centuries afterward, the book’s price had risen to over a million United States dollars, establishing the Book of Commandments “as one of the most expensive American books.”

Interestingly, two different versions of the book’s title page have survived. Most of the title pages in the extant copies of A Book of Commandments have a fleur-de-lis-style border around them. The rest have title pages without the border. One scholar has conjectured that the fleur-de-lis border was an experiment that was superseded by the plainer version.

The bindings that exist on the surviving copies represent diverse styles added at various periods. They range from rough leather bindings, like that on Wilford Woodruff’s copy, to fine bindings added by professional book binders.

Because the book was never finished, surviving copies end in the middle of one of the revelations. Wilford Woodruff had access to a complete copy of the partially printed revelation and added some blank pages to his Book of Commandments when he bound it. On those blank pages, he transcribed the rest of the revelation so that his Book of Commandments had a complete version of it. He then copied in another revelation, the Word of Wisdom, making his copy more complete than others.

Over the years, some have wondered what other revelations might have been included in the Book of Commandments if the printing project had gone forward to completion. Certainly the remaining verses of the partially printed revelation would have been included. The printers had also marked up nine other revelations for typesetting, and these probably would have been part of the book.

Three additional revelations had not yet been marked up but came from the same time period as the other revelations and seem likely candidates for inclusion. Finally, just as testimonies of witnesses to the Book of Mormon plates had been included in that printed book of scripture, a testimony of witnesses to the Book of Commandments had been prepared and almost assuredly would have been printed at the end of the volume.

Because only a small number of copies of the Book of Commandments survived, they hardly satisfied the growing need among members of the Church for access to Joseph Smith’s revelations. Those fortunate enough to obtain a copy of the Book of Commandments, of course, could read the revelations and share them with friends. But others could not. To go from a projected ten thousand copies, to a more practical three thousand, and finally to just a few dozen must have been a disappointment to many.
In the months that followed the destruction of the printing office and the disruption of efforts to publish the Book of Commandments, conditions among the Saints in Missouri only worsened.
